# Integrated Climate Governance

![An artistic rendering features interconnected torus figures positioned on a weathered wooden foundation emphasizing the relationship between environmental challenges and sustainable resolutions. This evocative scene highlights the pressing need for climate action, showcasing the potential for climate mitigation and a low carbon transition. A contrast with a clouded sky stresses the importance of embracing renewable energy and developing effective climate adaptation strategies.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/abstract-sculpture-illustrating-climate-resilience-and-sustainable.jpg "Abstract Sculpture Illustrating Climate Resilience and Sustainable Interconnectedness against the Backdrop of Climate Change with Wood Pattern Base")

## Fundamentals

The definition of **Integrated Climate Governance (ICG)**, at its most fundamental level, describes a paradigm shift in how societies and organizations approach climate action. It moves away from fragmented, sector-specific responses towards a holistic and interconnected framework. Imagine a traditional approach to environmental concerns as individual departments within a company working independently on [sustainability Meaning → Sustainability is the capacity for human civilization to coexist with the Earth's biosphere in a state of equilibrium.](https://sustainability-directory.com/)  initiatives.

ICG, in contrast, advocates for a unified strategy where these departments collaborate, share data, and align their goals under a common climate objective. This simple description highlights the core intention of ICG → to break down silos and create a cohesive, organization-wide response to the complex challenges of climate change.

Integrated Climate Governance represents a unified approach to climate action, moving beyond isolated efforts to create a cohesive and interconnected organizational response.

To further elucidate the meaning of ICG, consider its core components. Firstly, **integration** is key. This signifies the merging of climate considerations into all levels of decision-making and across all sectors. It is not simply about adding a ‘green’ layer to existing practices, but fundamentally rethinking operations, policies, and strategies through a climate lens.

Secondly, **governance** in this context extends beyond governmental actions. It encompasses the frameworks, processes, and institutions → both public and private → that guide climate-related decisions and actions. This broad delineation includes corporate strategies, industry standards, community initiatives, and international agreements. Therefore, ICG’s specification encompasses a wide spectrum of actors and actions.

![The weathered sphere underscores environmental degradation from climate change. Its interconnected framework signifies ecosystems facing global warming, needing urgent climate action and sustainable practices. Blue marks may represent diminishing water sources, demanding water conservation.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/up-close-look-at-weathered-sphere-showing-interconnection-to-environmental.jpg "Up Close Look at Weathered Sphere Showing Interconnection to Environmental Impact and Climate Resilience")

### Core Principles of Integrated Climate Governance

Understanding the meaning of ICG requires grasping its underlying principles. These principles act as guiding stars, shaping the implementation and effectiveness of integrated approaches.

The significance of ICG lies in its potential to enhance the effectiveness and efficiency of climate action. By moving beyond fragmented approaches, ICG aims to unlock synergies, reduce redundancies, and maximize the impact of resources invested in climate mitigation and adaptation. The import of this shift is particularly pronounced in complex, interconnected challenges like climate change, where isolated interventions are often insufficient to achieve meaningful and lasting change. The designation of ICG as a critical approach reflects its growing recognition as a necessary evolution in climate governance.

![Centered around the Earth’s sphere, the image displays interconnected loops reflecting ecological systems, environmental stewardship and climate change. Coral-like formations highlight biodiversity under ecological pressure urging towards sustainable development and renewable energy, reinforcing the importance of mitigation in discussions around resource management. This symbolizes conservation strategies in response to human activity underscoring environmental concerns impacting our planet as a symbol to create an ecologically sound framework and sustainability balance.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/abstract-climate-model-showing-interconnectedness-of-environmental-components.jpg "Abstract Climate Model Showing Interconnectedness of Environmental Components Related to Sustainability and Climate Change Impacts")

## Intermediate

Building upon the fundamental understanding of Integrated Climate Governance, we now move to an intermediate level of analysis, exploring its practical application and the challenges inherent in its implementation. The explanation of ICG at this stage necessitates a deeper examination of its operational meaning within organizational and societal contexts. ICG, interpreted through an intermediate lens, becomes less of an abstract concept and more of a concrete framework for action.

It’s about translating the definition of integration and holistic [governance Meaning → Governance, in an ESG context, refers to the systems of rules, practices, and processes by which a company is directed and controlled.](https://esg.sustainability-directory.com/)  into tangible strategies, policies, and operational changes within various sectors and organizations. This description moves beyond the ‘what’ and begins to address the ‘how’ of ICG.

Intermediate understanding of Integrated Climate Governance involves grasping its practical application, addressing implementation challenges, and translating abstract concepts into concrete actions within organizations and sectors.

![Abstract forms juxtapose ecological themes related to global warming and sustainable innovation. A blue ring signifies conservation alongside transparent elements showing environmental impact. Speckled patterns suggest air pollution while white segments evoke renewable energy initiatives and sustainable solutions.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/abstract-arrangement-reflecting-complex-systems-relevant-to-climate-change.jpg "Abstract Arrangement Reflecting Complex Systems Relevant to Climate Change Sustainability Solutions Innovation")

### Operationalizing Integrated Climate Governance

Operationalizing ICG requires a structured approach that considers various dimensions of integration. The elucidation of these dimensions is crucial for organizations seeking to adopt ICG principles. Consider these key aspects:

![This distressed sculptural globe, adorned with pointed projections, evokes a planet challenged by climate change impacts, including potential rising sea levels, extreme weather events, and habitat loss. The spikes may symbolize mitigation strategies promoting sustainability such as geoengineering, carbon capture technologies, and renewable energy sources. It challenges viewers to embrace climate action to counteract environmental degradation, promote greenhouse gas emission reduction, and enhance carbon sequestration efforts, striving towards climate restoration.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sculptural-globe-with-pointed-spikes-evoking-climate-change-tipping-points-and.jpg "Sculptural Globe with Pointed Spikes Evoking Climate Change Tipping Points and Environmental Resilience Visual Metaphor")

### Challenges in Implementing Integrated Climate Governance

Despite its conceptual appeal, implementing ICG in practice presents significant challenges. A thorough clarification of these obstacles is essential for realistic and effective ICG strategies.

One primary challenge is **siloed thinking and institutional inertia**. Organizations and government departments often operate in isolation, with established routines and vested interests that resist cross-sectoral collaboration. Overcoming this inertia requires strong leadership, clear mandates, and incentives for cooperation. Another hurdle is the **complexity of climate change itself**.

The multifaceted nature of climate impacts and the uncertainties associated with [future Meaning → Exploring future scenarios and possibilities is the core focus of the Prism sustainability satellite.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/)  climate scenarios make it difficult to develop comprehensive and robust integrated strategies. This complexity necessitates adaptive and iterative approaches to ICG.

**Data gaps and information asymmetries** also pose significant challenges. Effective ICG relies on access to reliable and comparable data across different sectors and scales. However, data availability and quality often vary significantly, hindering integrated analysis and decision-making. Furthermore, **power imbalances and conflicting interests** among stakeholders can impede the implementation of ICG.

Different sectors and actors may have competing priorities and resist efforts to integrate climate considerations if they perceive it as detrimental to their own interests. Navigating these [power Meaning → Power is the rate at which energy is generated and consumed, with a focus on transitioning to clean and renewable sources.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/)  dynamics requires inclusive and participatory governance processes.

Successfully implementing Integrated Climate Governance necessitates overcoming institutional inertia, managing complexity, addressing data gaps, and navigating power imbalances among diverse stakeholders.

Addressing these challenges requires a concerted effort to build capacity, foster collaboration, and develop innovative governance mechanisms. The denotation of success in ICG implementation is not merely about adopting integrated policies on paper, but about achieving tangible improvements in climate outcomes and building resilient and sustainable societies. The substance of ICG lies in its ability to drive real-world change.

![Architectural artistry features spiraling lines depicting solutions toward ecological balance and reduced carbon footprint promoting climate action and energy efficiency. The image employs contrast highlighting climate change challenges and innovations. Architectural commitment to renewable energy sources and environmentally friendly strategies is captured through shape.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/abstract-architectural-design-reflecting-sustainable-energy-innovations-climate.jpg "Abstract Architectural Design Reflecting Sustainable Energy Innovations Climate Change Adaptation and Environmental Responsibility Solutions")

## Academic

At an academic level, the meaning of Integrated Climate Governance (ICG) transcends a mere operational framework; it becomes a subject of critical inquiry, theoretical development, and empirical investigation. The academic interpretation of ICG delves into its epistemological foundations, its normative underpinnings, and its effectiveness in addressing the grand challenge of climate change. This level of analysis demands a rigorous and nuanced understanding, drawing upon diverse disciplines such as political science, environmental sociology, economics, and systems theory.

The explication of ICG in academia involves dissecting its complexities, interrogating its assumptions, and exploring its potential for transformative change. The academic statement on ICG seeks to define its boundaries, assess its efficacy, and refine its conceptual and practical contours.

Academic scrutiny of Integrated Climate Governance involves rigorous inquiry into its theoretical foundations, normative dimensions, and empirical effectiveness, aiming for a nuanced and critical understanding.

![Abstract sculpture highlighting ecological harmony symbolizes global sustainability amidst climate change, emphasizing the need for climate action and climate resilience. Circular and fluid forms blend, reflecting renewable resources and interconnected climate solutions for environmental protection. The balance represents mitigating climate impacts, demanding sustainable development, and embracing a low carbon footprint to achieve vital sustainable development goals and effective resource management in light of greenhouse gas emissions.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/abstract-sculpture-representing-environmental-balance-climate-resilience-and.jpg "Abstract Sculpture Representing Environmental Balance Climate Resilience and Sustainable Development in the Face of Climate Change")

### Redefining Integrated Climate Governance → An Academic Perspective

Through an academic lens, ICG can be redefined as a **complex adaptive system** of governance mechanisms, institutional arrangements, and societal practices designed to address climate change in a holistic, synergistic, and equitable manner. This academic definition emphasizes the dynamic and interconnected nature of ICG, recognizing it as a constantly evolving response to a constantly evolving problem. It moves beyond a static definition to capture the fluid and emergent properties of effective climate governance. The designation of ICG as a complex adaptive system highlights its inherent dynamism and interconnectedness.

Analyzing diverse perspectives on ICG reveals a spectrum of interpretations. Some scholars emphasize the **policy integration** aspect, focusing on the coherence and coordination of climate policies across sectors and levels of government. This perspective often draws upon policy analysis and public administration literature, examining the institutional mechanisms and policy instruments that facilitate or hinder integration. Other academic viewpoints highlight the **governance system** dimension, exploring the roles of different actors, the power dynamics at play, and the participatory processes that shape climate action.

This approach often draws upon political science and sociology, analyzing the social and political context of ICG. Still others focus on the **normative** dimensions of ICG, examining its ethical implications, its contribution to sustainable development, and its potential to promote social justice and equity in the face of climate change. This perspective engages with philosophy, ethics, and development studies, interrogating the values and principles that should guide ICG.

![This abstract sculpture promotes creative engagement with sustainable development goals illustrating environmental stewardship, carbon footprint consciousness, and mitigation efforts. The circular design symbolizes the circular economy and efficient resource management reducing environmental impact with eco friendly production. Featuring bands with weathered and blue textures represents the environmental impact of water systems, signifying climate risks of ecosystem degradation to emphasize nature based solutions and sustainable resource extraction policies.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/abstract-sculpture-embodiment-of-circular-economy-materiality-and-sustainable.jpg "Abstract Sculpture Embodiment of Circular Economy Materiality and Sustainable Art Reflecting Climate Resilience Initiatives")

### Cross-Sectoral Influences and Outcomes → A Deep Dive into the Water-Energy-Food Nexus

To illustrate the cross-sectoral influences and potential outcomes of ICG, consider the **water-energy-food nexus**. This nexus represents a critical intersection of three fundamental sectors that are deeply interconnected and highly vulnerable to climate change. The analysis of this nexus provides a concrete example of the complexities and opportunities inherent in ICG.

![The image reveals a stark scene of crystalline ice fragments on a shore showcasing the impact of climate change and a warming world. The visual embodies an urgency to address ecosystem integrity while working towards a more environmental friendly future. These transparent ice sculptures reveal the fragility of Arctic ice, the visual reinforces themes of water conservation due to melting ice caps which contributes to raising ocean levels and the necessity for environmental stewardship and sustainability practices to secure polar climate resilience.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/pristine-blue-ice-formation-and-climate-change-a-visual-reminder-of-arctic.jpg "Pristine Blue Ice Formation and Climate Change a Visual Reminder of Arctic Sustainability")

#### The Nexus Challenge

Climate change exacerbates pressures on water resources, [energy Meaning → The Energy domain delves into the transition from fossil fuels to renewable sources and the future of power generation.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/)  systems, and food production. For example, rising temperatures and changing precipitation patterns can reduce water availability for agriculture and energy generation. Simultaneously, energy production and food systems are significant contributors to greenhouse gas emissions, further fueling climate change. Fragmented governance approaches that address water, energy, and food in isolation are often inadequate and can even create unintended negative consequences.

For instance, promoting water-intensive biofuel production to reduce reliance on fossil fuels can exacerbate water scarcity and food security issues. This clarification underscores the limitations of siloed approaches.

![A two tone spherical sculpture, symbolic of Earth, sits atop a wood base. One half is beige, representing sustainability. The other is gray symbolizing ecological risks such as climate related impacts and pollution.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-art-a-symbol-of-environmental-responsibility-and-climate-awareness.jpg "Sustainable Art a Symbol of Environmental Responsibility and Climate Awareness Depicting a Spherical Sculpture on Wood")

#### ICG for Nexus Solutions

ICG offers a framework for addressing the water-energy-food nexus in a more integrated and sustainable manner. By adopting a nexus approach within ICG, policymakers and practitioners can:

The long-term consequences of failing to adopt ICG in the context of the water-energy-food nexus are potentially severe, including increased resource scarcity, food insecurity, social unrest, and environmental degradation. Conversely, successful implementation of ICG in this domain can lead to more sustainable resource management, enhanced climate resilience, and improved human well-being. The essence of ICG, therefore, lies in its capacity to facilitate transformative change towards a more sustainable and equitable future. The significance of this transformative potential cannot be overstated.

Integrated Climate Governance, particularly when applied to the water-energy-food nexus, offers a pathway to identify synergies, minimize trade-offs, enhance resilience, and promote equitable outcomes in the face of climate change.

# Towards a global governance framework: climate-driven migration

Examining research on the driving factors, patterns and consequences of climate-driven migration.

Share

Climate-driven migration – the movement of a person or groups of persons who, for sudden or gradual environmental reasons, leave their habitual place of residence – is increasing as a result of the climate crisis. It’s now critical that institutions acknowledge this phenomenon and update their climate adaptation strategies in response.  

This [report](https://www.graduateinstitute.ch/master-phd-programmes/towards-global-governance-framework) explores and examines existing research on the driving factors, patterns and consequences of climate-driven migration and its potential trajectories until 2050; generates an empirically-based trend analysis of how climate change is likely to drive population movements; and develops policy recommendations on how the global governance system can be adapted to meet this growing challenge.  

Recommendations, presented at the United Nations Summit of the Future, cover policy, funding and capacity-building and were formulated through secondary literature research and interviews with experts in the field. Recommendations include: 

The report is a collaboration between UNU-CPR and the [Geneva Graduate Institute](https://www.graduateinstitute.ch).

**Access ‘Towards a Global Governance Framework: Climate-Driven Migration’** [**here**](https://www.graduateinstitute.ch/master-phd-programmes/towards-global-governance-framework)**.**

Share

**Suggested citation:** Anaelle Deneuve, Sandra Prathap, Shiyuan Zhao and Anna Colabewala. *Towards a global governance framework: climate-driven migration* : UNU-CPR, 2024.

--- SOURCE 4: Global Policy and Collaboration - Climate Governance Initiative ---
URL: https://learn.climate-governance.org/climate-in-the-boardroom/globally-relevant-policies-and-processes

SUMMARY:
In this section you will learn about the global climate policies and processes designed to boost international cooperation in tackling the climate and nature

FULL CONTENT:
![Silhouette of a man jumping over a rock on a hillside](https://media.climate-governance.org/cdn-cgi/image/width=2048,format=auto/https://hub.climate-governance.org/media/images/francesco_califano_1_Hguxfz_TG_28_unsplash_11a6de200a.jpg)

# Global Policy and Collaboration

## Overview

Have you heard about the Paris Climate Agreement?

What about the international climate 'COP' sessions that make headlines from time to time?

In this section you will learn about the global climate policies and processes designed to boost international cooperation in tackling the climate and nature crises. These instruments have a profound impact on business. Your organisation's resilience and long-term viability depend on understanding and effectively engaging with climate and nature policies. The actions you take today will shape your organisation’s success amidst the ever-evolving climate policy landscape.

### What you need to know

Integrating climate and nature-related policies and processes into your organisation’s strategies is vital for long-term resilience and success.

Here are the main instruments that underpin global climate and nature policy:

![UNFCCC logo](https://media.climate-governance.org/cdn-cgi/image/width=2048,format=auto/https://hub.climate-governance.org/media/images/UNFCCC_logo_1_ca4c5001f5.png)

## The United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC)

### The United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change, or UNFCCC, is an international treaty that lays the foundation for worldwide cooperation on climate change.

![](https://media.climate-governance.org/cdn-cgi/image/width=2048,format=auto/https://hub.climate-governance.org/media/images/Convention_on_Biological_Diversity_logo_svg_18049ef7e2.png)

## The Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD)

### The Convention on Biological Diversity, or CBD, is an international treaty that promotes collaboration on all aspects of biological diversity.

Both conventions (the UNFCCC and CBD) have collaborated on a variety of work and established a joint working group to promote synergy. Given the common themes and objectives, there are calls for closer cooperation between the UNFCCC and the CBD.

![](https://media.climate-governance.org/cdn-cgi/image/width=2048,format=auto/https://hub.climate-governance.org/media/images/paris_agreement_logo_1140_5c123081a3.png)

## The Paris Agreement

### The Paris Agreement is an international binding treaty under the UNFCCC. Its goal is to limit global warming to well below 2°C above pre-industrial levels, and to pursue efforts to limit the temperature increase to 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels.

![](https://media.climate-governance.org/cdn-cgi/image/width=2048,format=auto/https://hub.climate-governance.org/media/images/KMGBF_Logo_1_23db82c40c.jpg)

## The Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF)

### The Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF) is an international treaty under the CBD. It aims to halt and reverse nature loss by 2030.

![Marrakech Partnership - Global Climate Action](https://media.climate-governance.org/cdn-cgi/image/width=2048,format=auto/https://hub.climate-governance.org/media/images/marrakech_e8d5a822a4.png)

## The Marrakech Partnership

### The Marrakech Partnership for Global Climate Action is a collaborative framework that opens the door to a world of extraordinary opportunities.

These frameworks allow for global discussions and collaboration between national governments, business, scientists and other stakeholders to tackle climate change together, and set the agenda for policy changes.

### Broader context

The IPCC, the international body for climate science highlighted in section B, reiterates there is a policy ‘emissions gap’ ¹, which shows that the current climate policies of all countries in the world are not sufficient to limit global warming to 1.5°C.  Climate Action Tracker (CAT) has developed a useful graphic that highlights the policy gaps in relation to the 1.5°C goal.

![](https://hub.climate-governance.org/media/images/CAT_2024_11_CAT_Thermometer_4_Bars_Annotation_9b777f1cc0.png)

Source: [Climate Analytics and New Climate Institute](https://climateactiontracker.org/publications/the-climate-crisis-worsens-the-warming-outlook-stagnates/)

### Businesses have an important role to play in helping to close this emissions gap. As a board director, you can help to drive this by:

![Green wall building](https://media.climate-governance.org/cdn-cgi/image/width=2048,format=auto/https://hub.climate-governance.org/media/images/jw_OF_Drwle_Ttd_E_unsplash_9bb9a72bce.jpeg)

Your organisation may have ambitious climate and nature goals, but if the industry associations you are involved with do not align with global climate and nature ambitions, supporting fossil fuels interests for instance, your GHG reduction goals could be considered as greenwashing.

Initiatives such as InfluenceMap and TransitionArc are mapping the climate targets, capital allocation, governance and policy advocacy of companies in different regions of the world, making it easier to visualise which companies are genuinely delivering climate action in contrast to greenwashing claims.

Now that you have a broader understanding of global policies and climate change projections, here are some ways you can contribute to make a difference.

![Electricity pylons](https://media.climate-governance.org/cdn-cgi/image/width=2048,format=auto/https://hub.climate-governance.org/media/images/jason_richard_0_Lqtb_RI_Fl_VQ_unsplash_0dc75ed7a1.jpg)

### How to take action

There is a general trend for increasing climate and nature policies and processes in different countries across the world as the urgency for action becomes increasingly understood. Many jurisdictions are implementing or considering both policy incentives and penalties for business to reduce their GHG emissions and take climate and nature action.  As a board director, you can play an important role in helping to encourage a stable climate and nature policy landscape globally and locally by participating in relevant consultations, industry and policy working groups and initiatives.

A growing number of ambitious initiatives for corporate climate and nature action highlight the critical role of business action in achieving positive climate and nature outcomes. Here are two examples:

![Race to Zero logo](https://media.climate-governance.org/cdn-cgi/image/width=2048,format=auto/https://hub.climate-governance.org/media/images/RTZ_RGB_PURPLE_2_ec3250ebdf.png)

## The Race to Zero

### The Race to Zero is a UN-backed global campaign mobilising non-state actors - companies, cities, regions, financial and educational institutions - to take immediate and robust action. Its aim is to halve global emissions by 2030 and deliver a healthier, fairer zero-carbon world.

![Race to resilience logo](https://media.climate-governance.org/cdn-cgi/image/width=2048,format=auto/https://hub.climate-governance.org/media/images/logo_rtr_pink_77fe9d0a38.webp)

## The Race to Resilience

### The Race to Resilience is the UN-backed global campaign to catalyse a step-change in global ambition for climate and nature resilience. It puts people and nature at the forefront in pursuit of a resilient world where we don’t just survive climate shocks and stresses, but thrive in spite of them.

--- SOURCE 5: Integrate mission governance into climate policy, says OECD ---
URL: https://www.globalgovernmentforum.com/integrate-mission-governance-into-climate-policy-says-oecd/

SUMMARY:
Governments should integrate mission governance into climate mitigation efforts to better drive progress towards green goals, the OECD has said.

FULL CONTENT:
# Integrate mission governance into climate policy, says OECD

![](https://www.globalgovernmentforum.com/wp-content/uploads/2025-04-10_global-warming_CREDIT-Marian-Anbu-Juwan-via-Pixabay-620x414.jpg)

Governments should integrate mission governance into climate mitigation efforts to better drive progress towards green goals, the OECD has said.

In its report – ‘Harnessing mission governance to achieve national climate targets’ – published last week, the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development recommended that governments leverage mission governance principles to meet their Nationally-Determined Contributions (NDC) under the Paris Agreement.

It noted that most countries are currently falling short of their climate commitments.

Global efforts to mitigate climate change “rely on the realisation of bold, ambitious, time bound and cross-sectoral national emissions targets”, the OECD said, adding that a traditional siloed governance approach is “ill-suited to tackling this complex task”.

It said missions – which are clearly defined overarching policy objectives aimed at tackling a societal challenge within a specified timeframe and have a clear mission statement and underlining targets – could help ensure that a whole-of-government and whole-of-society approach is adopted that is “appropriate to the scale of the challenge”.

Read more: [The missing link: exploring the potential of national climate institutions](https://www.globalgovernmentforum.com/the-missing-link-exploring-the-potential-of-national-climate-institutions/)

## Operationalising national climate targets

The report analyses recent assessments from 15 countries’ climate councils against a set of mission governance principles: structure, strategic orientation, co-ordination, execution, and resources.

The countries assessed include Australia, Canada, Chile, Denmark, Germany, Mexico, New Zealand, South Africa and the UK.

Mission-driven innovation in government is intended as a break from a conventional focus, instead centring on problem-specific complex challenges, with collaboration a priority.

Read more: [‘It’s a break from the norm’: how moonshot approaches can drive government innovation](http://globalgovernmentforum.com/its-a-break-from-the-norm-how-moonshot-approaches-can-drive-government-innovation/)

Governments including the UK have worked to [implement mission-based approaches to policy delivery](https://www.globalgovernmentforum.com/enjoy-the-ride-uk-missions-driving-a-more-agile-civil-service-say-speakers-at-innovation-2025/), but the OECD review found that where missions are in place, they are rarely used to achieve overarching climate goals, and are instead “constrained to narrower domains” such as within science, technology and innovation policy. As such, they are “disconnected from the wider climate efforts and lack the necessary reach, legitimacy or influence for the needed transformational impact”.

To remedy this situation and bridge the gap between missions and national climate targets, the OECD proposes a “multi-level mission-oriented approach”. This would address “both the overarching mission inherent to the NDC, as well as efforts to meet the more narrowly defined sub-targets that typically underpin them”.

Read more: [UN hails leap forward in global climate action transparency](https://www.globalgovernmentforum.com/un-hails-leap-forward-in-global-climate-action-transparency/)

## ‘Not just a challenge, but an opportunity’

The report said a successful missions approach should improve horizontal and vertical co-ordination, aligning plans and resources more effectively across government levels and agencies; integrate climate considerations into core government processes such as budgeting and procurement; and cultivate societal engagement and enduring political support leading to “more citizen participation and consistent policies resistant to short-term fluctuations”.

Developing necessary capabilities throughout the mission lifecycle would also mean that “critical capacity gaps in climate action” are addressed, while the promotion of continuous adaptation and learning could “enhance the agility and effectiveness” of climate strategies.

It also said that the mission approach’s emphasis on encouraging private sector investment “could help multiply the effect of public funding in climate action”.

It is recognised in the OECD Action Plan on Governing Green – endorsed by member countries in 2022 – that “innovative approaches to governance are needed that embrace complexity, help overcome administrative segmentation, and mobilise societal actors”.

Climate change “presents not just a challenge but an opportunity to refine and improve our governance systems over time,” the OECD said.

Read more: [World breaches 1.5C warming limit as climate deadline looms for governments](https://www.globalgovernmentforum.com/world-breaches-1-5c-warming-limit-as-climate-deadline-looms-for-governments/)

#### About Mia Hunt

Mia has been editor of globalgovernmentforum.com since 2019. She has 15 years’ experience as a journalist and editor and specialises in writing for civil and public servants worldwide, including covering sustainability policy and related issues. She has led the Global Government Women’s Network since it launched in 2023.
Previously, she covered commercial property having been market reports and supplements editor at Property Week and deputy editor at Retail Destination.
She graduated from Kingston University London with a first-class honours degree in journalism and was part of the team that produced The River newspaper, which won Publication of the Year at the Guardian Student Media Awards in 2010.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Integrated Climate Governance (ICG) promotes a holistic framework, merging climate strategies across sectors to enhance effectiveness, reduce redundancies, and foster sustainable practices.

International organizations like the UNFCCC facilitate global cooperation, providing essential frameworks and guidelines that shape global climate governance and promote collective action against climate challenges.
Multilateralism is crucial for addressing climate change, enabling diverse stakeholders to collaborate, share resources, and harmonize efforts towards achieving shared climate goals effectively.
